retrieved_docs.title: Copper Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Many instances of copper toxicity are often the result of accidental consumption or installation of contaminated water sources, copper salt-containing topical creams for burn treatments, acidic foods cooked in uncoated copper cookware, or in suicide attempts; the lethal dose of ingested copper is 10-20 g. Copper sulfate is a readily available chemical in many countries and is often sold over-the-counter. It is commonly used in farming as a pesticide in the leather industry and for making homemade glue. The burning of copper sulfate in houses and shops (as a good luck charm and for some religious activities) is a common practice among Buddhists and Hindus. The bright blue color of the hydrated form of copper sulfate crystals is alluring to children and is a frequent reason for inadvertent poisoning. [6] Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by excessive copper accumulation, resulting from a mutation in the gene encoding a copper-ATPase enzyme. [7] Copper in the blood exists in 2 forms: bound to ceruloplasmin (85% to 95%), and the rest "free," loosely bound to albumin and other small molecules.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Properties of Metal Modified GIC
retrieved_docs.content: Chemical bonding to the tooth structure is slightly lessened due to the silver present in the composition. Both silver alloys admixed GIC and cermet release fluoride in significant quantities. However, the fluoride release is comparatively less in cermet than in silver alloy admixed GIC. Metal-modified GICs have improved handling properties and exhibit fewer porosities in set cement.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Melanoacanthoma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of oral melanoacanthoma is yet to be determined, but it is mostly associated with a traumatic process that stimulates melanocyte activity. [3] This is supported by the reactive infiltrate of eosinophils found on histopathology, which corroborates the reactive nature. A review of 56 articles of 115 cases concluded that 77% of authors found an association with a reactive etiology. [1] Chronic trauma, or chemical irritants, stimulates melanocytes, which result in either oral pigmentation or a melanoacanthoma. Regular contact with petroleum derivatives found in toothpaste and mouthwashes may act as an irritant agent. These derivatives include sodium lauryl sulfate, phenolphthalein, nitrophenol, chlorophenol, phenylenediamine sulfate, amine fluoride, and cocamidopropyl betaine. Hydrogen peroxide mouthwashes can also irritate the oral mucosa and cause melanoacanthoma. Silver amalgam has also been described as an etiologic factor that may cause morphologic changes and pigmentation, and dental restoration with other materials. [1] Bruxism, biting of the cheeks, poorly fitted removable prosthesis, implant surgery, and non-specific chronic trauma have been reported features preceding the presentation of an oral melanoacanthoma. Other etiologic factors include patients treated for chronic asthma and ferrous lactate for chronic treatment of iron-deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Heavy Metal Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Proper discharge of effluents from factories.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Metal Modified GIC
retrieved_docs.content: Metal particles in the silver alloy are added to the conventional GIC powder to improve the mechanical properties of GIC and permeate its use in Class I cavities in molar teeth. The GIC can be reinforced with metal in a silver admixture or cermet mixture.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Reinforced Cement
retrieved_docs.content: Metal reinforced GIC: the mixture of amalgam powder and GIC, commonly known as 'miracle mix.' [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Stainless steel crowns are the most commonly used restorative option for repairing and preserving the remaining tissue of severely damaged and decayed teeth (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown). They were introduced into pediatric dentistry in 1947, first described by Engel, and then popularised by Humphrey in 1950. Stainless steel crowns have outperformed other materials, such as amalgam and composite, in terms of durability and longevity for more than a half-century. In fact, no restorative material has provided the benefits of low cost, reliability, and durability when interim full-coronal coverage is required. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Heavy Metal Toxicity -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Although classical symptoms and signs for each heavy metal exist, they are not present in every case, and a high index of suspicion is necessary for a correct diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Lipofuscin -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Metals such as iron, copper, zinc, aluminum, manganese, and calcium make up only 2% of lipofuscin. [11] Despite minimal contribution to lipofuscin’s total volume, iron is hypothesized to be the principal source free radicals via Fenton mechanics. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Mo99 - Tc99m Generator -- Clinical Significance -- Disposal of Molybdenum-Technetium Generator
retrieved_docs.content: Label removal: All labels indicating Caution—Radioactive Material must be removed from the generator before disposal with regular medical waste.

retrieved_docs.title: Copper Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the importance of early recognition of copper toxicity in improving patient outcomes. Identify the potential risk factors for copper toxicity. Compare the first and second-line treatments available to patients with copper toxicity. Describe how comprehensive, high-quality patient education delivered by an interprofessional team plays a critical role in preventing copper toxicity and reducing related complications. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Burning Mouth Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is an association with certain irritants, including dental materials such as mercury, amalgam, methyl methacrylate, cobalt chloride, zinc, and benzoyl peroxide. [9] Also, certain food allergies, including peanuts, sorbic acid, chestnuts, and cinnamon, have been related to BMS. [10] As previously stated, there is a connection with patients with neuropsychiatric conditions such as major depression, chronic anxiety, and mood disorders. The most common association is with a major depressive disorder, and it may follow acute symptoms or share an association with a comorbid condition at some point in the patient's life. [3] Other causes include orthodontic appliances, possible prescription drug adverse effects, increased bradykinin, and comorbid dermatologic conditions. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- History and Physical -- Mean Corpuscular Volume Below 80 fL
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with lead exposure are frequently exposed to lead paints, notably children in older homes where lead-based paints are common. Adults with lead exposure may report occupational exposures such as mining, pipefitting, auto repair work, and ceramic glazing. Lead poisoning can present with characteristic lead lines on the gums and metaphyses of long bones, along with symptoms such as abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy (most commonly affecting the fibular and radial nerves), and fatigue. In severe cases, children can develop encephalopathy. Sideroblastic anemia can occur as a result of lead poisoning or chronic alcohol use, serving as the underlying etiology in some cases. Studies have shown a correlation between a low (pre-surgical) MCV and advanced stages (III or IV) of endometriosis. [18] These lower indices are believed to reflect dysregulated iron metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Silver Alloy Admixed GIC
retrieved_docs.content: The powder of this modification of GIC is obtained by adding 1 part of spherical silver alloy particles to 8 parts of conventional GIC powder. The liquid consists of polyacrylic acid. The powder and liquid are mixed at a ratio of 3:2 by weight. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Mo99 - Tc99m Generator -- Clinical Significance -- Disposal of Molybdenum-Technetium Generator
retrieved_docs.content: Radioactive waste management: If the generator still contains residual radioactivity, it should be managed as low-level radioactive waste. This process requires adherence to institutional protocols for radioactive waste disposal, which may include transferring the waste to a licensed radioactive waste disposal facility. [25] [26]

By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Ascorbic acid and calcium together form a specific formulation that is classified as a vitamin C combination, indicating that this pairing is recognized as one of the various combinations that incorporate vitamin C (ascorbic acid) with calcium to potentially enhance nutritional or therapeutic effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: Ascorbic acid (vitamin C), including combination formulations, is classified as a type of plain vitamin C product.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: formulations containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide and cupric oxide) are classified as vitaminy, illustrating that both single‑nutrient supplements and compound formulations belong to the same hierarchy. Inverse‑ISA statements integrate combined entities such as “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two” and “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂” into the vitaminy class. Minerals are closely linked to vitamins; multivitamins that contain minerals are considered vitamins and are associated with the mineral group, which is a subset of selenium—a trace element that connects to broader heavy‑metal categories. Nutritional deficiency (نقص التغذية) is associated with both vitamins and minerals, emphasizing the clinical importance of adequate intake.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2379
retrieved_docs.content: The VITAMIN B COMPLEX with iron and zinc tablet includes zinc as one of its ingredients, indicating that zinc is a component of this multivitamin formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B1, plain is recognized as a form of vitamin B1 that may be administered alone or together with vitamins B6 and B12, indicating that it can be used both as a single‑nutrient supplement and as part of combination vitamin preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Niacin (vitamin B3) is a constituent of the B‑vitamin family, which classifies it as a member of the broader B Vitamin Family. In a wide range of oral multivitamin and mineral formulations, niacin is listed as an active ingredient, indicating its therapeutic role in these products. Specifically, the supplement “ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg / CHOLECALCIFEROL 25 µg / .ALPHA‑TOCOPHEROL ACETATE 100.5 mg / PHYTONADIONE 80 µg / THIAMINE 3 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3.4 mg / NIACIN 40 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 25 mg / FOLIC ACID 999 µg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 120 µg / BIOTIN 600 µg / CALCIUM PANTOTHENATE 20 mg / CHOLINE 20 mg / IODINE 150 µg / ZINC 15 mg / SELENIUM 140 µg / COPPER 1 mg / MANGANESE 2 mg / CHROMIUM 120 µg / MOLYBDENUM 77 µg ORAL CAPSULE [PRO HERS VANILLA]” contains niacin as an active component. The capsules “ASCORBIC ACID 25 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 2 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACIN 1.8 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 25 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12.5 µg / BIOTIN 300 µg / CALCIUM

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide and cupric oxide. Such products are used to prevent nutritional deficiency, support pregnancy, and provide comprehensive micronutrient repletion. Antianemic preparations form a therapeutic subclass that specifically pairs vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, hydroxokobalamin) with folic acid to treat megaloblastic (macrocytic) anemia. They are distinguished from iron‑based antianemic agents, which rely on iron as the primary active ingredient. An oral tablet “folic acid 1 mg / vitamin B12 0.5 mg” exemplifies the vitamin B12‑and‑folic‑acid complex. IRON/MULTIVITAMINS represent a heterogeneous group of iron‑fortified multivitamin‑mineral supplements that belong simultaneously to the multivitamin, multivitamin + mineral, and “multivitamins with minerals”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and manganese is noted, suggesting formulation considerations. Collectively, these multivitamin‑iron and zinc products aim to improve hematopoiesis, correct simultaneous micronutrient deficiencies, enhance iron absorption (often facilitated by vitamin C and chelated iron forms), reduce gastrointestinal side effects, and provide comprehensive metabolic support for patients at risk of iron‑deficiency anemia, folate‑deficiency anemia, combined nutritional deficits, or those requiring bariatric supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 806
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A is a widely incorporated component in a diverse array of pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products. In many oral multivitamin formulations—such as those containing ASCORBIC ACID 125 mg/CHOLECALCIFEROL 10 µg/THIAMINE HYDROCHLORIDE 1.5 mg/RIBOFLAVIN 1.7 mg/NIACINAMIDE 20 mg/PYRIDOXINE 12.5 mg/FOLIC ACID 1000 µg/CYANOCOBALAMIN 1000 µg/BIOTIN 300 µg/CALCIUM PANTOTHENATE 10 mg/VITAMIN A ACETATE 1500 µg and similar products (e.g., BETA CAROTENE 600 µg/Vitamin A Palmitate 600 µg, various prenatal and chewable multivitamins, and DHA‑enriched capsules) vitamin A is listed as an active moiety or active ingredient, often supplied as vitamin A acetate, retinyl palmitate, or beta‑carotene. These preparations also combine vitamin A with other vitamins (C, D, E, B‑complex), minerals (iron, zinc, copper, selenium, magnesium, manganese, chromium, molybdenum), and nutraceuticals (fish oil, lutein, alpha‑lipoic acid), reflecting its role in comprehensive micronutrient supplementation. Vitamin A

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: Mineral supplements are classified both as calcium‑protein preparations and as molybdenum‑related supplements, indicating that they encompass formulations that combine calcium with protein components while also providing the essential trace element molybdenum. This dual categorization reflects the broader role of mineral supplements in delivering multiple essential nutrients to support metabolic and physiological functions.

The combined presence of other B‑vitamins, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin E, essential fatty acids, minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine), probiotics, and docusate further enhances the therapeutic scope of these formulations, promoting overall micronutrient repletion, gastrointestinal health, and patient well‑being.
